name: Healthcare & Clinical

users.context: Administers medication, records vitals and assessments, executes care plans across a bay of patients. Constantly interrupted, moves between rooms, documents on a workstation-on-wheels or mobile device, often wearing gloves.
users.expertise: Clinical expert; high domain knowledge, variable IT fluency. Operates under time pressure and cognitive load where a documentation error can harm a patient.

users.context: Reviews charts, places orders (CPOE) for medication, labs, imaging, writes notes, reviews results, and makes diagnostic/treatment decisions. Time-boxed per patient, mobile across wards or clinics.
users.expertise: Deep clinical expertise; low tolerance for click-heavy ordering and alert fatigue.

users.context: Verifies medication orders for interactions, allergies, dose, and renal adjustment before dispensing; manages formulary and controlled substances.
users.expertise: Expert in pharmacology and drug-interaction logic; relies on clinical decision support but must catch its gaps.

users.context: Rapidly assesses arriving patients, assigns acuity (e.g., ESI level), and prioritises the queue. High-stakes, fast, frequently interrupted, with incomplete information.
users.expertise: Expert in triage protocols; needs speed and unambiguous prioritisation cues.

users.context: Abstracts diagnoses and procedures into ICD-10/CPT codes from clinical documentation for reimbursement and reporting. Detail-intensive, deadline-bound.
users.expertise: Expert in coding standards and payer rules; needs traceability from code back to documentation.

users.context: Governs access to protected health information, audits record access, manages consent and breach response.
users.expertise: Expert in HIPAA/privacy law and audit controls.

workflows.name: Medication administration (eMAR / five rights)
steps: Pull due medications for the patient
Scan patient wristband to confirm identity
System checks right patient/drug/dose/route/time and allergies
Document administration, refusal, or hold with reason

workflows.name: Emergency triage and acuity assignment
steps: Record vital signs and quick assessment
Apply triage protocol to assign acuity level
Re-assess if waiting time exceeds threshold

workflows.name: Computerised provider order entry (CPOE)
steps: Select patient and verify context (patient, encounter)

workflows.name: Clinical documentation / encounter note
steps: Open encounter and review prior history
Document history, exam, assessment, and plan
Reconcile problem list, allergies, and medications
Release for coding and continuity of care

risks: Wrong-patient error from selecting the wrong chart or context bleed across open records
Medication error (wrong drug, dose, route, time) reaching the patient
Missed drug allergy or interaction due to overridden or absent alert
Alert fatigue causing clinicians to dismiss a genuinely critical warning
Critical lab value not acknowledged or acted upon in time
Unauthorised access to PHI or snooping without legitimate need
Documentation lost when an interruption discards an unsaved note
Triage acuity mis-assigned, delaying care for a deteriorating patient
Copy-forward/cloned note propagating outdated or incorrect clinical data
Incorrect ICD-10/CPT code causing claim denial or compliance exposure
environment_constraints: Interruption-heavy: clinicians are routinely interrupted mid-task
Shared workstations and workstations-on-wheels with rapid user switching
Gloved hands and infection-control limits on touch surfaces
Time pressure with high patient-to-staff ratios
Bright clinical lighting and bedside/dim-room variation
Mobile use across wards with intermittent Wi-Fi
24/7 shift work with handover continuity needs
domain_patterns: Patient-context banner persistent across every screen
Clinical decision support alerts with override-with-reason
audit_compliance: HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules (US PHI)
GDPR for EU patient data (special-category data)
21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures for regulated records
ICD-10 / CPT coding and CMS billing compliance
accessibility_concerns: Persistent, unmistakable patient-identity banner to prevent wrong-patient errors
Alerts must distinguish severity without colour alone (icon + text + ranking)
Large touch targets usable with gloves at the bedside
Legible at varying distances and lighting (dim rooms to bright wards)
Fast keyboard/voice paths for high-frequency documentation
Screen-reader support for clinicians with disabilities; no information conveyed by colour only
anti_patterns: Allowing multiple patient charts open such that orders cross contexts
Burying allergy/interaction warnings or making override frictionless and unlogged
Excessive low-value alerts that train clinicians to click-through (alert fatigue)
Auto-saving copy-forward notes that perpetuate stale data
Discarding an in-progress note on interruption/timeout without recovery
Hard session timeouts mid-administration with no safe resume
Hiding who accessed a record, defeating privacy audit
